skill.name: expo-tailwind-setup
skill.description: Set up Tailwind CSS v4 in Expo using react-native-css and NativeWind v5 for styling across iOS, Android, and web.
skill.summary: Configure Tailwind CSS v4 and NativeWind v5 for universal Expo styling.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, environment, and obfuscation patterns, but most matches are Markdown examples or benign Expo configuration snippets. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscated payload, or malicious execution intent was found. The remaining publishable risk is supply-chain exposure because the guide tells users to install preview and nightly packages from the npm ecosystem.

medium_findings.title: Preview and Nightly Dependency Installation
medium_findings.description: The guide instructs users to run an npm-based Expo install command that includes NativeWind preview and react-native-css nightly versions. This is legitimate setup guidance, but preview and nightly packages can change quickly and increase supply-chain and stability risk for user projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installation command is explicit and includes preview or nightly dependency versions. The risk is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Code Blocks Trigger Command Patterns
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and fenced code examples. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes shell commands outside user-visible setup instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown examples and prose, not active skill code. They are intended for the user to copy into an Expo project.

low_findings.title: Benign Environment Variable Platform Check
low_findings.description: The environment access finding refers to process.env.EXPO_OS, which is used to choose web versus native CSS variable behavior. I found no evidence of secret access, environment file reads, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact variable is EXPO_OS, a platform selector used in Expo builds. It is not a secret-bearing environment variable.

low_findings.title: Benign Example URL
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL finding points to an example image source using example.com in a UI snippet. I found no evidence of network exfiltration or a hidden remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domain is example.com inside a visible Image usage example. No credentials or project data are sent to that URL.

low_findings.title: False Positive Static Heuristics
low_findings.description: The dynamic import, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and dangerous combination findings are not confirmed by semantic review. The cited lines contain a TypeScript type annotation, frontmatter text, platform CSS media queries, or troubleshooting prose rather than malicious logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows ordinary Expo and CSS documentation. I found no prompt injection, obfuscation, reconnaissance intent, or cryptographic implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Package Installation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes an npx expo install command that downloads packages from the JavaScript package ecosystem. This is expected for the skill, but users should verify package versions before running it in production projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible and directly related to setup. The risk depends on package registry trust and dependency version stability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Platform Environment Check
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample wrapper code reads process.env.EXPO_OS to select native or web CSS variable behavior. This is not secret access, but it is still environment-variable use that should stay limited to platform checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context clearly identifies EXPO_OS as a platform condition. No sensitive environment variables are referenced.

content.value_statement: Expo projects need consistent styling across native and web targets. This skill provides a focused setup path for Tailwind CSS v4, NativeWind v5, and react-native-css.
actual_capabilities: Lists the dependencies needed for Tailwind CSS v4, NativeWind v5, and react-native-css in Expo.
Shows Metro and PostCSS configuration files for the Expo styling pipeline.
Provides CSS-wrapped React Native components for className support.
Explains global CSS imports, custom theme variables, and platform-specific styles.
Shows how to use Apple semantic colors with CSS variables.
Documents migration differences from NativeWind v4 and Tailwind v3.
limitations: Uses preview and nightly package versions that may change behavior over time.
Does not verify that every dependency version works with every Expo SDK version.
Provides guidance and snippets, not an automated installer or migration script.
Requires users to adapt paths and imports to their project structure.

use_cases.title: Start a new Expo styling setup
use_cases.description: Configure a new Expo app with Tailwind CSS v4, NativeWind v5, and reusable CSS-enabled components.

use_cases.title: Modernize an existing NativeWind project
use_cases.description: Replace older Babel and Tailwind v3 patterns with a CSS-first setup for newer NativeWind guidance.

use_cases.title: Standardize cross-platform UI tokens
use_cases.description: Add theme variables and platform-specific CSS values for iOS, Android, and web targets.

prompt_templates.title: Install the setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Expo project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to set up Tailwind CSS v4 in my Expo app. Show the files I need to create and the dependency command.

prompt_templates.title: Review my current config
prompt_templates.scenario: Check an existing project before changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my Expo Metro, PostCSS, and Babel files against this Tailwind setup. Tell me what to keep, remove, or change.

prompt_templates.title: Create wrapped components
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a project-specific tw component folder using this skill. Include wrappers for View, Text, ScrollView, Pressable, TextInput, Image, and Animated View.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced migration from older NativeWind or Tailwind patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a migration plan from NativeWind v4 and Tailwind v3 to this Expo Tailwind v4 setup. Include risks, test steps, and rollback notes.

output_examples.input: Set up Tailwind CSS v4 in a new Expo app.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step setup plan with dependency installation, Metro configuration, PostCSS configuration, global CSS imports, and basic usage guidance.

output_examples.input: My styles are not applying after migration.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering CSS entry imports, wrapped components, Metro configuration, and removed NativeWind Babel settings.

output_examples.input: Add iOS semantic colors to my Tailwind theme.
output_examples.output: A CSS variable plan that maps Apple platform colors for iOS and fallback light-dark values for web and Android.

best_practices: Pin dependency versions and test the setup after each Expo SDK upgrade.
Keep CSS-enabled wrapper components in one shared folder for consistent imports.
Verify styling on iOS, Android, and web because platform CSS support differs.
anti_patterns: Do not keep old NativeWind Babel presets when using this Tailwind v4 setup.
Do not assume preview or nightly packages are stable for production without testing.
Do not mix wrapped and unwrapped React Native components when className behavior must be consistent.

faq.question: Does this skill install packages automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and file examples that a user or agent can apply in an Expo project.

faq.question: Does it require a Babel config?
faq.answer: No. The guide says Tailwind v4 and NativeWind v5 should use the Metro and CSS-first setup instead.

faq.question: Can it help with web and native targets?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance covers iOS, Android, and web styling patterns for Expo projects.

faq.question: Why are wrapper components needed?
faq.answer: react-native-css needs explicit wrappers so className values can map to native style props.

faq.question: Is this safe for production projects?
faq.answer: It can be used with care, but preview and nightly dependencies should be reviewed and tested before production use.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
